task: I’ve got a subcontractor dispute brewing over who’s on the hook for a damaged section, and I want to handle it the right way before it turns into a bigger headache. What should I gather and put in writing first so I’m not boxed in later?

turns.agent_response:
Before you start assigning blame, gather and preserve the facts in a way that shows exactly **what happened, when, where, and who had control**. The goal is to avoid making statements that limit your options later.

### Gather first
- **Photos/video** of the damaged area from multiple angles, with dates if possible
- **Before-and-after condition evidence**: prior photos, inspection reports, punch lists
- **Daily logs / field reports** for the relevant dates
- **Schedule records** showing who was working in that area and when
- **Contract documents**:
  - prime contract
  - subcontract(s)
  - scope of work exhibits
  - change orders
  - site-specific responsibilities
  - warranty or indemnity clauses
- **Delivery tickets / material records** if materials may be involved
- **Emails, texts, RFIs, meeting notes** related to the area or issue
- **Witness names and statements** from supervisors, foremen, and anyone who saw the damage or work in progress
- **Inspection/QA reports** and any third-party reports
- **Access/control records** if relevant: who had possession of the area, lockout/tagout logs, permits, barricades, etc.

### Put in writing immediately
Create a **neutral incident memo** or internal note that includes:
- date/time the damage was discovered
- exact location
- description of the damage
- who was present
- what work was underway
- what you observed firsthand vs. what others reported
- steps taken to secure the area and prevent further damage
- request to preserve evidence

Keep the language factual and avoid:
- admitting fault
- speculating about cause
- accusing a subcontractor without proof
- promising payment or repair responsibility

### Best wording style
Use phrases like:
- “At this time, we are investigating…”
- “Based on current information…”
- “No determination of responsibility has been made…”
- “Please preserve all records, photos, and materials relating to the affected area…”

### Also do this
- **Send a preservation notice** to all potentially involved parties
- **Notify your insurer or bond company** if required
- **Check notice deadlines** in the contract so you don’t waive rights
- **Limit verbal discussions**; follow up important conversations in writing

### If you want the safest next step
I can draft a **neutral preservation notice** or **incident report template** you can send today. If you share the basic facts, I’ll keep it factual and non-committal.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the right track. I’d want it tighter, though.

First thing: lock down the **timeline** and **control**. Who had the area, who was scheduled there, who actually showed up, and who signed off on what. That’s what matters.

Put together:
- photos, before and after
- daily report for the day before, day of, day after
- subcontract scope and any change orders
- inspection status, hold points, and sign-offs
- emails/texts/RFIs about that area
- names of everyone on site who saw it
- any delivery or equipment records if that could be the cause
- the exact time it was discovered and who discovered it

Then write one clean incident memo. No drama. No guessing. Just facts:
- what was found
- where
- when
- who was in the area
- what work was underway
- what got secured after discovery

And don’t say a subcontractor caused it unless you’ve got hard proof. I’ve seen that blow up fast. Last time something got ahead of the paperwork on my side, the only thing that saved us was having the logs and inspection trail lined up. Same deal here.

If you want, I can draft the actual memo language or a notice to the sub. Keep it short and solid.
